coredata.dc:title: One-step green synthesis of biomass-derived graphene quantum dots as a highly selective optical sensing probe
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Abbas, A.
Rubab, S.
Rehman, A.
Irfan, S.
Sharif, H.M.A.
Liang, Q.
Tabish, T.A.
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained a significant amount of research interest due to their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, incredible fluorescence, and intriguing physicochemical characteristics. However, the fundamental issues, such as acidic contamination and expensive product price, are still obstacles to their commercialization. In this study, we present an eco-friendly one-step approach to produce GQDs using biomass waste green precursor and relatively green solvent ‘ethanol’. This approach simultaneously addresses the issues of contaminants from strong acids and the high cost initiated by costly precursors. The results reveal that GQDs demonstrate a size range of 0.5–4 nm and a thickness of 1–3 layers of graphene. As-prepared GQDs exhibit considerable surface grafting and exceptional optical properties with a high quantum yield of 21%. These GQDs are used as a fluorescence probe to detect ferric ions (Fe3+) owing to their exclusive optical properties. A precise and selective sensor is developed with a detection limit down to 0.5 μM. This work identifies the importance of using a relatively green process and an inexpensive biomass precursor to produce high-quality GQDs that hold a promising future for use in photocatalytic, bioimaging, and practical sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Biomass waste
Green synthesis
Sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic demonstration of the synthesis process of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from biomass waste. Spent tea leaves are hydrothermally treated at 200 °C to form turbostratic carbon which further develops into aromatic carbon and subsequently produces bright blue fluorescent GQDs over a duration of 8–12 h.
Ultraviolet–visible (UV-Vis) spectra of GQDs-8 and GQDs 12, displaying strong absorption in the UV range.
Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra at a range of excitations from 300 to 400 nm for (a) GQDs-8, (b) GQDs-12. Abbreviation: GQDs, graphene quantum dots.

(a) Particle size distribution, (b, c) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images at low and high magnification and (d) high-resolution TEM image showing lattice fringes of GQDs-12, inset is the FFT of GQD. Abbreviations: FFT, fast Fourier transform; GQDs, graphene quantum dots.
(a) AFM image of GQDs-12 and (b) corresponding line profile of the line marked in (a), indicating a height of 0.5–0.8 nm. Abbreviations: AFM, atomic force microscopy; GQDs, graphene quantum dots.

FTIR spectra of spent tea leaves powder and GQDs-12 indicating a substantial change in the surface functional groups from precursor to product. Abbreviation: FTIR, Fourier transform infrared; GQDs, graphene quantum dots.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-12 solution in the existence of various metal ions at 340 nm excitation. (b) Column graph to compare the emission intensity of GQDs in the absence and presence of different metal ions. (c) The affinity trend of a range of metal ions toward GQDs. Abbreviation: GQDs, graphene quantum dots.

Fluorescence spectra of graphene quantum dots (20 μg/mL) with Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM, with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm and (b) linear regression plot of fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration in low concentration range of 0–6 μM.

head.ce:title: One-step green synthesis of biomass-derived graphene quantum dots as a highly selective optical sensing probe

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained a significant amount of research interest due to their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, incredible fluorescence, and intriguing physicochemical characteristics. However, the fundamental issues, such as acidic contamination and expensive product price, are still obstacles to their commercialization. In this study, we present an eco-friendly one-step approach to produce GQDs using biomass waste green precursor and relatively green solvent ‘ethanol’. This approach simultaneously addresses the issues of contaminants from strong acids and the high cost initiated by costly precursors. The results reveal that GQDs demonstrate a size range of 0.5–4 nm and a thickness of 1–3 layers of graphene. As-prepared GQDs exhibit considerable surface grafting and exceptional optical properties with a high quantum yield of 21%. These GQDs are used as a fluorescence probe to detect ferric ions (Fe3+) owing to their exclusive optical properties. A precise and selective sensor is developed with a detection limit down to 0.5 μM. This work identifies the importance of using a relatively green process and an inexpensive biomass precursor to produce high-quality GQDs that hold a promising future for use in photocatalytic, bioimaging, and practical sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thanks to their interesting properties and promising potential, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained a significant amount of study interest. The urgent demand to produce GQDs on a wide scale could be accomplished by renewable, economical, and environmentally friendly biomass resources. GQDs, which are composed of a single or a small number of graphene layers, have drawn huge interest owing to their unique physicochemical characteristics and potential commercial uses. They are excellent choices for replacing conventional semiconductor quantum dots due to their higher solubility, tunable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and ease of functionalization [1–3]. It is highly desirable to produce these superior GQDs on a large scale for a wide range of applications, including bioimaging [4–6], biosensors [7,8], photocatalysis [9–11], solar cells [12], and light-emitting diodes [13]. Hence, it is highly desirable to produce high-quality, cost-efficient, and renewable GQDs on a large scale.

Given the numerous published reports on this topic, the synthesis of GQDs is still on its infancy [14]. GQDs have been produced using a variety of carbon sources through acid oxidation [15,16], hydrothermal treatment [17–19], microwave treatment [20], electrochemical method [21], pyrolysis [22], and carbonization [23]. In addition to graphite, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, carbon fiber, citric acid, glucose, and coal are some of these sources. Typically, the majority of techniques used to make GQDs are limited to laboratory-size operations, expensive starting ingredients, and/or utilizing strong acids [24,25]. Highly acidic treatment poses challenges and delays to the overall fabrication process. Shin et al. developed an acid-free process for producing GQDs; however, it was limited to using commercially synthesized precursors and produced very little amount of the finished product [26]. Nonetheless, the finished product is still too expensive for ultimate common usage. To accomplish this goal, we propose an acid-free eco-friendly approach for large scale production of GQDs from inexpensive precursor ‘biomass waste’ that has already proved its potential for producing numerous carbonaceous materials [27–29].

The development of heavy metal ion sensors for environmental and biological systems is highly desired [30,31]. GQDs have been used to construct a variety of fluorescence sensors because of their unique optical properties [32–34]. The identification and removal of Fe3+ have attracted a lot of interest among various metal ions due to the key role of iron in ecological systems and human lives. Monitoring the Fe3+ content in patients with Parkinson's disease is essential because iron accumulation in their neurons was revealed [35]. The same way Fe3+ being one of the primary water contaminants leads to environmental pollution [36]. Therefore, it is necessary to precisely measure, record, and monitor the iron concentration in environmental and biological systems [37]. These objectives can be achieved by using the GQDs’ PL quenching effect produced by Fe3+ [38,39]. The simple and robust fluorescence-based sensor of GQDs makes it an efficient tool for monitoring hazardous metallic ions in the environment.

Herein, we propose a method that uses only ethanol as a solvent to directly produce GQDs from biomass waste without the need of any oxidizing, reducing, or passivizing agents. This has made viable the production of GQDs from biomass waste in one step. Hydroxyl (OH•) radicals can be generated from ethanol in a controlled manner by simply controlling the processing temperature to govern the redox potential. These radicals working together with high temperature are the key basis for the cleavage of carbon-carbon bonds. The carbon domain in the waste biomass might frequently disintegrate during this process. The repeated processes of oxidation, cleavage, and reduction transform the carbon domains into small-sized GQDs. These GQDs are highly suitable for sensing applications because of their remarkable optical properties. As evidence of the concept, these GQDs are used as a fluorescence sensor to detect the traces of Fe3+ with very promising detection limits.

Spent black tea bags were collected from a nearby tea shop. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethanol (C2H5OH), and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich and used without any further changes. The polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) microporous filtration membranes (0.1 μm and 0.025 μm) were supplied by Merck Millipore, UK. 1kD MWCO dialysis bags were used to purify GQDs (Spectrum Labs, item number 132640). TEM grids with ultra-thin carbon films (CF-400-Cu-UL, Electron Microscopy Sciences, USA) and mica discs (AGF7013, Agar Scientific) were used for the morphological analysis. Deionized (DI) water was used to make all the solutions.

The spent tea bags were thoroughly washed in DI water to remove organic substances (such as color), and then they were allowed to air dry for a day. The spent tea leaves were removed from paper tea bags, and the paper bags were discarded. The samples were then oven-dried for a further 10 h at 90 °C to remove the remaining moisture. A commercial grinder was used to reduce the dried components to a fine powder (<100 μm). About 1 g of the tea powder was mixed with 100 mL of ethanol and the mixture was then sonicated for 30 min. Following that, the mixture was placed in an autoclave lined with Teflon and heated for variable lengths of time (8 h and 12 h) at a fixed temperature of 200 °C. The reactor was then allowed to gradually cool to room temperature once the heater was turned off. The resulting brown solution was filtered across a 0.1 μm PVDF microporous membrane and dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 3 h to remove any contaminants, followed by second filtration across a 0.025 μm PVDF microporous membrane. As a result, GQDs were extracted from dialysis bags that were light yellow. Although still not certain, the solution's pale-yellow color under visible light is a sign that GQDs have been formed [20,40]. The GQDs prepared at 200 °C for 8 h are designated as GQDs-8 and GQDs obtained over a reaction duration of 12 h are denoted as GQDs-12.

The material structure, morphology, and properties were characterized using a range of analytical techniques. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI, and operated at 300 kV) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were used to evaluate the shape, size and microstructure of the samples. ImageJ Software (1.46r) was used to analyze TEM images to calculate the diameter and size distribution of GQDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to observe the electronic characteristics of the samples. The FTIR spectra were recorded using a Bio-Rad FTIR spectrometer with a Diamond ATR.KBr tip (Cary 630 FTIR, Agilent Technologies). The Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorophotometer and the Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer were used to record the absorption and fluorescence spectra, respectively. The thickness of the GQDs was determined using atomic force microscopy (AFM, XE-15, Park), and the number of graphene layers was determined from AFM images using the ‘Gwyddion 2.55’ software.

Bright blue luminescent GQDs were prepared from green biomass waste using a relatively green solvent in an eco-friendly approach. The schematic demonstration of the synthesis process is presented in Fig. 1 .

The GQDs were prepared in a single step using spent tea leaves. The conversion of tea leaves into GQDs is a multistage catalytic process because it involves the separation of crystalline and amorphous cellulose parts and then conversion of crystalline cellulose into turbostratic (irregular) carbon nanocrystals, followed by the development of aromatic carbon. However, hydrothermal process successfully performs all the reaction in a single pot. The crystalline cellulose present in the precursor has a great capability of converting into graphitic carbon. The high temperature and pressure, along with the generation of H+ and OH− ions, are responsible for the graphitization to take place. The development of graphitic structure occurs through the scission of cellulosic chains and subsequent polymerization by intramolecular redisposition to form turbostratic carbonaceous structure, which eventually leads to the establishment of a graphitic aromatic structure [41,42]. Moreover, there is great role of OH• radicals in the cleavage of graphitic domains into the nanosized GQDs.

By controlling the reaction time and temperature, the generation of hydroxyl radicals was controlled to produce subnanometer scale GQDs. We carried out two different batches of experiments to evaluate the generation of enhanced hydroxyl radicals with the reaction time. As will be discussed later, the results revealed that with increase in reaction time to 12 h, the substantial amount of hydroxyl radicals was generated, leading to high-quality GQDs. As a comparison, pure DI water was also employed as a solvent, but only minimal conversion was obtained. Hence, it was proved that ethanol is responsible for the generation of hydroxyl radicals and formation of GQDs.

UV–Visible (UV-Vis) and PL spectroscopies were used to investigate the optical characteristics of GQDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs-8 and GQDs-12 are presented in Fig. 2 , which reveal significant absorption in the UV range. A wide absorption band in the range of 250–300 nm can be clearly noticed. Interestingly, GQDs-12 exhibit a stronger absorption compared to its counterpart GQDs-8, which demonstrates its superior optical characteristics. The absorption band at about 300 nm is associated with n-π∗ transition in carbonyl group attached to the GQDs, which is typically observed in graphene-like systems. The unpaired electrons on the oxygen atom of the carbonyl group (CO) are known as the nonbonding or “n’ electrons. The transition from n to π∗ takes place when one of the nonbonding ‘n’ oxygen electrons is propelled into the antibonding π∗ orbital. These results suggest that the surface oxygen states (CO) are responsible for the UV absorbance in these GQDs. The bandgaps of GQDs-8 and GQDs-12 were determined to be 5.45 and 5.5 eV, respectively, using Tauc plot of the absorbance spectra [36].

The optical properties of GQDs were further studied by PL spectroscopy. Fig. 3 shows the optical properties of GQDs examined by critically investigating the PL emission at different wavelength ranges. When the GQDs are excited by a 300 nm laser, they demonstrate emission at about 400 nm. The Stokes shift takes place as the emission spectrum is larger than the excitation wavelength. The GQDs' PL intensity increases with excitation wavelength, with the strongest peak appearing when 330 nm is being used as the excitation wavelength. The GQDs synthesized in this work show excitation-based photoluminescent emission because of unique structural groups causing multiple emissive states [20,32,39].

The emission maxima shift as the excitation wavelength changes. When the emission spectrum is increased from 300 to 400 nm, the emission peak position changes and exhibits a red-shift of about 45 nm. The presence of a large number functional groups on the surface or GQDs of various sizes explains this red shift [43]. In order to get further interpretation and insight into the optical properties of GQDs, their quantum yields were determined with reference to quinine sulfate. In a specific protocol, the absorbance values and unified PL intensities of GQDs are compared with that of quinine sulfate to calculate the quantum yield. The quantum yields of GQDs-8 and GQDs-12 were recorded as 16.5% and 21%, respectively.
Based on the pronounced and attractive optical properties, GQDs-12 were selected for further examination and application studies.

TEM microscopy was used to analyze the shape and size of the GQDs-12 produced. The TEM images are presented in Fig. 4 . A uniform nanosize distribution and uniform shape and size of the GQDs-12 can be clearly observed (Fig. 4a and c). The exact size and homogeneous dispersion of the quantum dots can be noticed without any aggregation. Fig. 4b shows the precise size measurement of the as-synthesized GQDs-12, which display an average size distribution of 2.3 ± 0.3 nm.

The HRTEM image in Fig. 4d demonstrates prominent lattice fringes indicating a good crystallinity of the GQDs prepared. A lattice plane spacing of 0.238 nm was calculated related to (11
2 ¯
0) plane of graphene (0.24 nm) [43]. The inset in Fig. 4d shows a fast Fourier transform (FFT) image of a single GQD. The FFT image also indicates good crystallinity of the GQDs.

In order to learn more and get further insight into the topographic morphology of the GQDs prepared, the AFM analysis was performed. The AFM is the best-known approach to evaluate the thickness of low-dimensional materials, especially graphene and its derivatives. The AFM image depicted in Fig. 5 a reveals the topographic morphology of GQDs-12. Based on the corresponding height profile of the line marked in AFM image, the thickness of as-prepared GQDs-12 was calculated. As shown in Fig. 5b, the thickness was recorded to be in the range of 0.5–0.8 nm that is related to 1–3 layers of graphene. It is interesting to note that the GQDs-12 prepared in this study are very thin discs of graphene consisting of only a couple of layers. These results further substantiate the graphene nature of these GQDs in contrast with their carbon quantum dots counterpart which are spherical particles of carbon with a flock of carbon layers.

FTIR spectroscopy was used to examine the structural and electric characteristics of as-synthesized GQDs-12. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 6 demonstrate that GQDs possess surface with abundant oxygenated functional groups. In comparison with tea leaves precursor, the GQDs demonstrate the presence of specific functional groups, such as hydroxyl (O–H) at 3200-3600 cm, carbonyl (CO) at 1630 cm, carboxyl (C–O) at 1380 cm), and alkyl (C–O) at 1050 cm [44,45]. It is important to note that aliphatic groups present in precursor at 2800-2900 cm and below 800 cm have been almost completely removed in the GQDs. Mainly the aromatic groups and hydroxyl/carboxyl groups were retained in GQDs. Numerous hydroxyl/carboxyl groups on the surface of GQDs give them hydrophilic properties and make them stable and easily dispersible in water.

As a result of abundant surface functionalization and prominent optical properties, these GQDs can be applied to design a selective and sensitive sensor for environmental systems and biological monitoring.

As previously mentioned, as-synthesized GQDs have a strong fluorescence and many surface functional groups, which provide them hydrophilicity and enable them to be soluble in water. Therefore, these GQDs can be used as a novel type of optical sensing probe because of their excellent solubility, powerful optical characteristics, and outstanding surface grafting [32–34,46]. In the current work, sensitive and selective sensing of Fe3+ was studied by examining the quenching behavior of recently synthesized GQDs. Several metal ions, including Ag1+, Al3+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Mo2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, and Li1+ were added to the GQDs solution (20 μg/mL) to investigate the PL quenching behavior. A 100 μM solution of various metal ions was added to a solution of GQDs, the PL emission spectra at 340 nm excitation were obtained after 2 min of incubation. Fig. 7 a shows that most of the metal ions maintained the stability of the PL emission spectra of the GQDs solution. The PL emission of GQDs was significantly reduced when Fe3+ ions were introduced, showing a strong quenching tendency of Fe3+. The intensity ratio (F/Fo), which measures the PL intensity of GQDs in the presence and absence of various metal ions, was calculated to assess the quenching ability of different metal ions. According to Fig. 7b, among the 14 different metal ions, only Fe3+ significantly lowers the intensity ratio of GQDs when compared to the control sample (blank, GQDs solution without any metal ions). These results specify a strong selective ability of GQDs for detecting Fe3+.

To fully understand the strong quenching behavior of Fe3+, it is essential to understand the quenching mechanism. The surface functional groups for which Fe3+ has a high affinity are the hydroxyl/carboxyl groups that are present on the surface of GQDs. A stable molecule is formed as a result of the hydroxyl/carboxyl groups' attraction to Fe3+ [36]. When an excited electron partially moves toward the d orbital of Fe3+ instead of releasing photons, the fluorescence quenching takes place [28,32]. The quantitative affinities of various metallic ions for GQDs were studied under identical experimental conditions. According to the results, Fe3+ had the maximum affinity for GQDs out of the 14 distinct metal ions (Fig. 7c). These results imply that these GQDs have a bright future in selective Fe3+ practical detection.

To further understand the mechanism, a Stern-Volmer plot was produced (Fig. S1, Supplementary Information) based on equation (F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q]) [47]. In this equation, F0 and F are the recorded fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher (Fe3+), respectively. [Q] describes the concentration of quencher and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant. In present work, a linear relation was obtained between the fluorescence intensity and quencher concentration and K
SV was calculated to be 13,620 M₋1. The Stern-Volmer plot suggests the typical static quenching mechanism by forming complexes between the functional groups on GQDs and quencher Fe3+ [36].

Sensitivity and selectivity are essential elements in sensing applications. The effect of various Fe3+ concentrations on the PL intensity of GQDs was fully investigated to analyze the sensitivity of the GQDs-based sensor. Fig. 8 a illustrates how the addition of Fe3+ in various concentrations (1–100 μM) changed the PL intensity of the GQDs solution. A steady decrease in PL intensity is observed when the amount of Fe3+ gradually rises. Fe3+ concentration increases with an increase in PL quenching. These results demonstrate that GQDs are sensitive to the detection of Fe3+ and that Fe3+ has a significant quenching efficiency. According to the equation (Fo– F)/Fo, a calibration curve for quenching efficiency of Fe3+ is shown in Fig. 8b. With an increase in Fe3+ content, the calibration curve shows a significant increase in quenching efficiency.

Meanwhile, it was observed that the quenching efficiency and Fe3+ concentration have a strong linear relation. Even at extremely low concentrations, Fe3+ can be identified correctly because of the attainment of a linear interpolation value (R2) of 0.993. These results suggest that these GQDs can be used as a highly sensitive and focused probe for Fe3+ detection. Using the formula LOD =
3
σ S
, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined, where s is the slop of the linear calibration curve and σ is the standard deviation of the intercept. In this study, the LOD was measured to be 0.5 μM. The LOD for Fe3+ is less than the numerous previous studies [34,39,48]. The LOD obtained in the present work is also significantly lower than the World Health Organization's recommended limit (5.36 μM) for the amount of Fe3+ in drinking water [49]. Additionally, the iron content measured in PD patients were in the range of 565–820 μg/L [50]. The LOD of present sensor ‘0.5 μM’ is comparable to ∼28 μg/L, which is considerably lower than the minimum limit of iron accumulation in PD patients. Therefore, we believe our sensor is also suitable for monitoring trace levels of iron in PD patients.

Due to their high fluorescence, high purity, excellent water solubility, and abundant surface grafting, these GQDs show extremely low LOD for the detection of Fe3+. These results suggest the present GQDs-based sensor's remarkable sensitivity for identifying Fe3+ residues in environmental and biological systems. The zero-cost precursor, inexpensive solvent employed, fast preparation of GQDs in single step, high quality of the product obtained, and proof-of-concept application as a practical sensor lend further credence to our work for commercialization.

The synthesis of GQDs has been successfully achieved from biomass waste in an acid-free, economical, environmentally friendly, and sustainable manner. The current approach excludes the possibility of contamination from strong acids and the high cost associated with the use of expensive precursors. The results suggested that GQDs were created with a size distribution of 0.5–4 nm with an average of 2.3 ± 0.3 nm. The thickness of GQDs was recorded to be 0.5–0.8 nm, corresponding to 1–3 layers of graphene. As-prepared GQDs exhibit distinctive optical properties while having a good quantum yield of up to 21%. These GQDs were further used as a fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection owing to their unique optical properties. To detect Fe3+ precisely, a sensor was developed with high sensitivity up to 0.5 μM. The advantages of high quality, inexpensive, sustainability, and the ability to scale-up for producing high-grade GQDs on a commercial scale are demonstrated by this simple yet successful approach. The current work highlights the necessity of utilizing waste biomass resources to create valuable nanomaterials for real-world applications.

Aumber Abbas: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review and editing. Shabnum Rubab: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review and editing. Abdul Rehman: Software, Data curation, Formal analysis. Syed Irfan: Software, Writing – review and editing. Hafiz Muhammad Adeel Sharif : Data curation, Writing – review and editing. Qijie Liang: Writing – review and editing. Tanveer A Tabish: Software, Data curation, Writing – review and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely appreciate financial assistance from open research fund of Songshan Lake Materials Laboratory, China (2021SLABFN21), Newcastle University, UK (OSR/0530/SD16/ABBA), University of Oxford, UK and University of Sargodha, Pakistan.

The following is the supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2023.101555.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.N. Baker, G.A. Baker, Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights, Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 49 (2010) 6726-6744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pee-dots: biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots derived from the upcycling of urine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.B. Essner, C.H. Laber, S. Ravula, L. Polo-Parada, G.A. Baker, Pee-dots: biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots derived from the upcycling of urine, Green Chemistry, 18 (2016) 243-250.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile and simple method for synthesis of graphene oxide quantum dots from black carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Lu, C. Wu, D. Liu, H. Wang, W. Su, H. Li, Y. Zhang, S. Yao, A facile and simple method for synthesis of graphene oxide quantum dots from black carbon, Green Chemistry, 19 (2017) 900-904.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Large-scale simultaneous synthesis of highly photoluminescent green amorphous carbon nanodots and yellow crystalline graphene quantum dots at room temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.L. Liu, L. Yang, R.S. Li, B.B. Chen, H. Liu, C.Z. Huang, Large-scale simultaneous synthesis of highly photoluminescent green amorphous carbon nanodots and yellow crystalline graphene quantum dots at room temperature, Green Chemistry, 19 (2017) 3611-3617.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gram-scale synthesis of single-crystalline graphene quantum dots with superior optical properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, Y. Wang, T. Xu, H. Liao, C. Yao, Y. Liu, Z. Li, Z. Chen, D. Pan, L. Sun, Gram-scale synthesis of single-crystalline graphene quantum dots with superior optical properties, Nat. Commun., 5 (2014) 1-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhu, Q. Meng, L. Wang, J. Zhang, Y. Song, H. Jin, K. Zhang, H. Sun, H. Wang, B. Yang, Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging, Angewandte Chemie, 125 (2013) 4045-4049.
